title: Jasminum kerrii
intro: Jasminum kerrii, also known as Kerrii jasmine, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae, native to Southeast Asia. The health benefits of Jasminum kerrii include its potential to reduce anxiety and stress, improve sleep quality, and possess antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which are responsible for its medicinal and aromatic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Kerrii jasmine include teas, infusions, and extracts, which are often used in traditional medicine to promote relaxation and reduce inflammation.
taxonomy: Jasminum kerrii is classified within the family Oleaceae and the genus Jasminum. It is a species of flowering plant. The classification is part of the larger system of taxonomy that categorizes living organisms based on shared characteristics and evolutionary relationships. Further classification within the plant's kingdom, phylum, class, and order is as follows: kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, and order Lamiales. This classification helps to understand the plant's relationships and evolutionary history.
uses: Jasminum kerrii, commonly known as the golden jasmine, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant is said to possess antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its medicinal uses. In some cultures, Jasminum kerrii is used to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and skin conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using the plant for medicinal purposes. Its leaves and flowers are often used in herbal remedies, but caution should be exercised due to potential side effects.

The plant has been traditionally used in various cultures to treat a range of health issues, including anxiety, stress, and insomnia. side_effects: Jasminum kerrii, also known as the Kerrii Jasmine or Golden Jasmine, is a flowering plant native to Southeast Asia. While there is limited information available on its side effects, it's generally considered non-toxic and safe when handled properly.
